result.markdown:
# Point Patterns



## Big Idea
Space matters: Spatial pattern lets us understand process. That is, knowing where things are (and whether their locations are random, clustered, or repulsed) is the first step to understanding the underlying mechanisms that put them there.

We can quantify the pattern of points themselves or in the presence of an explanatory variable.

There are two kinds of spatial structure we care about in PPA. First-order effects describe how intensity varies across space: are some areas simply denser than others, regardless of what the neighboring points are doing? Second-order effects describe the interactions between points at various distances: do nearby points attract or repel each other? Kernel density estimation gets at the first question. Ripley's K gets at the second. Keeping that distinction in your head makes the rest of this chapter easier to follow.

That said, the two are not always easy to separate. If you see a cluster of points, is it because intensity is genuinely higher in that area, or because points attract each other? The conventional answer is that interaction tends to operate at small scales while large-scale variation in density reflects intensity. So if clustering only shows up at short distances, think second-order. If the whole map looks uneven, think first-order. In practice you often need both tools to tell the story.

::: {.callout-note}
Before diving in, make sure you're comfortable with distance matrices. If you need a refresher, work through the [Distance Matrix aside](03xAsideDistanceMatrices.html).
:::


## Packages
We will use `spatstat`[@R-spatstat] for point pattern analysis and I've thrown in some tidyverse[@R-tidyverse] syntax as well.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
library(spatstat)
```
:::


## Point Patterns
Point pattern analysis (PPA) is the study of the spatial arrangements of points in space. It fundamentally deals with whether or not points are randomly distributed, clumped, or repulsed from each other.

PPA doesn't require any other measurement besides the location of points. The easiest way to think about this is to imagine a scatter plot of the points (i.e., a simple point map of the locations). Quantifying the pattern of these points is the goal of the analysis and we do so over the boundary of the data which is called the bounding box, or the convex hull of the points.

What we want to know is whether a bunch of points are randomly arranged or if they have some kind of spatial structure. We call a random pattern complete spatial randomness (CSR) and model it as a Poisson point process (which has convenient mathematical properties discussed in the reading). If a pattern doesn't look like CSR we are generally interested in whether the points are clumped or repulsed. But we can't just eyeball it. Our brains make patterns where there are none. Thus, we'll look at two methods for determining CSR: kernel estimation and Ripley's K. As always we hope to make inference about process (e.g., contagion or competition) by understanding pattern.

Point patterns are a relatively simple form of spatial analysis but can really make clear concepts like clustering and uniformity.

### Simulated Example
Let's illustrate. I'll generate four maps. Each has 100 points. Your job is to figure out if the pattern is: random, clumped (clustered), or uniform (repulsed). Note that the code that goes into making these patterns is using random normal or random uniform distributions to accomplish this. I often simulate patterns in data that I know exist and then see if I can detect them using some method. If you look at `simDat` below you'll see that it is four `data.frame` objects bound together. Each one shows a different pattern.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n <- 100
patternA <- data.frame(x=rnorm(n),y=rnorm(n),id="A")
patternB <- data.frame(x=runif(n),y=runif(n),id="B")
patternC <- data.frame(expand.grid(x = seq(0, 1, length.out = sqrt(n)),
                                   y = seq(0, 1, length.out = sqrt(n))),
                       id="C")

patternD <- data.frame(expand.grid(x = rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = n/20),2),
                                   y = rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = n/20),2)),
                       id="D")
patternD$x <- jitter(patternD$x)
patternD$y <- jitter(patternD$y)

simDat <- bind_rows(patternA,patternB,patternC,patternD)

simDat <- simDat %>% group_by(id) %>% 
  mutate(x = scales::rescale(x), 
         y=scales::rescale(y))

ggplot(simDat,aes(x=x,y=y)) + 
  geom_point() + coord_fixed() + 
  facet_wrap(~id)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


So, looking at the various plots, can you classify them? E.g., are the points random? Are they clustered? Are they repulsed? Or perhaps both?

### Data
Plant ecologists (and astronomers and cell biologists) love point patterns. Tree stem locations are ideal data for exploring spatial patterns. We'll look at three different data sets of tree locations. When you load the data below make sure to summarize, look at `str` of the objects, and plot them. Note that all of these are `ppp` objects. The `spatstat` package uses the `ppp` class as a handy way of storing this type of spatial data. We will use it here and we could certainly convert other spatial data (e.g., shp files) to `ppp` objects as needed.

Below we will look at data using the `japanesepines`, `redwood`, and `bei` data sets. You should look at the help file for each, e.g., `?japanesepines`, to learn more.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data(japanesepines)
data(redwood)
data(bei)

summary(japanesepines)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Planar point pattern:  65 points
Average intensity 65 points per square unit (one unit = 5.7 metres)

Coordinates are given to 2 decimal places
i.e. rounded to the nearest multiple of 0.01 units (one unit = 5.7 metres)

Window: rectangle = [0, 1] x [0, 1] units
Window area = 1 square unit
Unit of length: 5.7 metres
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(redwood)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Planar point pattern:  62 points
Average intensity 62 points per square unit

Coordinates are given to 3 decimal places
i.e. rounded to the nearest multiple of 0.001 units

Window: rectangle = [0, 1] x [-1, 0] units
                    (1 x 1 units)
Window area = 1 square unit
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(bei)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Planar point pattern:  3604 points
Average intensity 0.007208 points per square metre

Coordinates are given to 1 decimal place
i.e. rounded to the nearest multiple of 0.1 metres

Window: rectangle = [0, 1000] x [0, 500] metres
Window area = 5e+05 square metres
Unit of length: 1 metre
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(japanesepines)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(redwood)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-2.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(bei)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-3.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Note that the units on these data are not the same. The `japanesepines` data are measured where one unit equals 5.7 meters, `redwood` is unitless (re-scaled at some point to an xy grid ranging from 0,1), `bei` is actually in meters in a 1000x500m bounding box. This doesn't really matter when we interpret each data set on its own, but we should keep it in mind when comparing numbers down below.

::: {.callout-note}
Note also that I'm using the `plot` function to show the point data. E.g., `plot(japanesepines)`. Because `japanesepines` is class `ppp`, the generic function `plot.ppp` is being used with the authors of `spatstat` having made the visualization choices for you. Look back at the [Understanding Methods and Generics in R aside](02xAsideMethodsAndGenerics.html). 
:::

If you are a die hard `ggplot` enthusiast you can figure out how to make your own plot using `ggplot`. E.g., you can get the units right and geek out on this as much as you like by looking at the plumbing of the `japanesepines` object which is a `list`. I'm not suggesting you do this. But I know many of you take your data viz seriously and might be offended by the default plots.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tibble(x=japanesepines$x * japanesepines$window$units$multiplier, 
           y=japanesepines$y * japanesepines$window$units$multiplier) %>%
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x=x,y=y)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(x=paste("x (",
               japanesepines$window$units$plural,
               ")",sep=""),
       y=paste("y (",
               japanesepines$window$units$plural,
               ")",sep="")) +
  coord_fixed() + theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


### Kernel Density Estimation
One way of looking for patterns with points is visually. We can do this in an intuitive way by smoothing the data by applying a kernel density estimation (KDE). KDE is a non-parametric method for estimating the probability density function of a variable. Try this for an idea of what we are going to do with spatial data:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
x <- rnorm(n)
hist(x,freq=FALSE)
lines(density(x),col="red")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Or for `ggplot` folks. I'll stop doing the double plotting now but want to make everybody aware that you can plot any way you choose.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot() + 
  geom_histogram(mapping = aes(x=x,after_stat(density)),fill="grey") + 
  geom_density(mapping = aes(x=x))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Ok. What is this? We took `n` random points (`n=100`from above) and estimated the probability density function with a kernel. Kernel density estimation is a basic data-smoothing technique and the density estimates are closely related to histograms as you can tell.

The key to using kernels is selecting the bandwidth. The bandwidth of the kernel is a parameter which indicates the degree of smoothing. When we plot a histogram, we need to consider how many bins to break the data into. Choosing the bandwidth of the kernel is a Goldilocks problem. We want one that is just right. Too small and your kernel is spiky (not enough smoothing) and too big leads to over smoothing. There is no perfect answer for how smooth to make your kernel, although there is much gnashing of teeth about getting a "good" answer.

With point patterns we basically want to plot the `x` data and the `y` data against each other and look for patterns in the intensity of the points. Let's leave the bandwidth issue alone for now and look at some plots.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
jp.den <- density(japanesepines)
summary(jp.den)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
real-valued pixel image
128 x 128 pixel array (ny, nx)
enclosing rectangle: [0, 1] x [0, 1] units (one unit = 5.7 metres)
dimensions of each pixel: 0.00781 x 0.0078125 units
(one unit = 5.7 metres)
Image is defined on the full rectangular grid
Frame area = 1 square units
Pixel values
	range = [25.17565, 133.178]
	integral = 63.61766
	mean = 63.61766
```


:::
:::


This density object (class `density.ppp`) has methods for `summary`, `plot`, `contour` and `persp`. E.g., here is a perspective plot:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
persp(jp.den,theta = 30, phi = 30)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


You can add contour lines and even the add points back in after you plot the KDE and gander at it:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(jp.den)
contour(jp.den,add=TRUE)
points(japanesepines,pch=20)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


You can run the same for the others too:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(density(redwood)) # points(redwood,pch=20)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(density(bei)) # points(bei,pch=20)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


These are plots of the intensity of the points. They show the expected density and thus the units are points per unit area. If the plot surface has peaks it means that intensity is a function of spatial location. This is a non-parametric estimate and the function above `density.ppp` selects the bandwidth of the kernel for you. If you want to look at different bandwidths change the argument `sigma`. For instance try values of 0.1, 1, 10 like so `plot(density(bei,sigma=10))`.

There are ways of parametrically estimating the intensity of a point pattern by identifying the specific function for the intensity with parameters fit by maximum likelihood estimation. In practice, I've never seen this done but rather see people using kernels to get a feel for the order in the pattern. So what can we see in the above figures? Which patterns look random, which look clumped?

## Ripley's K
The kernel approach is pretty and mucking about with the bandwidth gives you an idea as to what distances are important in driving pattern. But it would be nice to quantify at what distances a point pattern conforms to, or deviates from, CSR. The reading describes at least three ways of quantifying whether a pattern fits CSR at a given distance. These include the $\hat{G}$ function (which measures the distance to the nearest event) and $\hat{F}$ function (which measures the distance from a point to the nearest event) but we are going to use the Cadillac of point pattern tools: Ripley's K. Ripley's K is a statistic for detecting deviations from spatial homogeneity. Its estimate is:

$$\hat{K}(r)=\frac{\lambda^{-1}}{n-1} \sum_{i \neq j} I(d_{ij}<r)$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the distance between points $i$ and $j$, $n$ is the number of points, $r$ is a radius, $\lambda$ is the mean density of points, and $I$ is the indicator (aka characteristic) function that has a value of one if its operand is true and zero otherwise. The $K$ function measures the number of events found up to a given distance ($r$).

Ripley's K describes the point processes at many distance scales. This is an attractive property of the statistic because many environmental point patterns can cluster at one distance and become random or uniform at another. 

We can test $\hat{K}$ against CSR which has a value of $K(r)=\pi r^2$ for a homogeneous Poisson process. We are going to do this with the `envelope` function.

We can calculate an envelope of possible values of $K(r)$ in a Monte Carlo framework where the number of simulations specifies the significance level as $\alpha = 2/(1 + n)$ where $n$ is the number of simulations. If the observed function value lies outside the envelope we can reject the null hypothesis of CSR at the distance $r$. With the default of 99 simulations you have $\alpha = 2/(1+99) = 0.02$. 

A heads up though. That significance level is technically only valid if you pick a single value of $r$ before looking at the data and test only there. In practice nobody does that -- you look at the whole curve. When you do that you are implicitly running many tests at once (one per value of $r$), which is the same problem as running a bunch of t-tests without correcting for multiple comparisons. Your true $\alpha$ is higher than advertised. The fix is to use `global=TRUE` in the `envelope` call, which builds the envelope based on the maximum deviation of each simulated curve across all $r$ values rather than pointwise. This gives you a single test of the whole curve at $\alpha = 1/(1 + n)$ with the default `nrank=1`. For a deeper treatment see @baddeley2014tests who go after this problem directly.

And even if you do use `global=TRUE` and get a clean test, keep in mind that a p-value only tells you whether the pattern is distinguishable from CSR. It doesn't tell you why. Two different processes can produce identical patterns, and a non-significant result doesn't mean the process is random, just that you couldn't detect structure with this data and this statistic. The goal is always to use pattern to think about process, not to collect p-values.

The `envelope` function is chatty by default and prints out information on the simulations as it runs them. In the code chunk below I'll quiet it down by setting the argument `verbose=FALSE`. If you leave it as the default value of `TRUE`, the markdown doc gets messy.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n <- 100
japanesepinesK <- envelope(japanesepines, fun = Kest, nsim = n, verbose=FALSE)
beiK <- envelope(bei, fun = Kest, nsim = n, verbose=FALSE)
redwoodK <- envelope(redwood, fun = Kest, nsim = n, verbose=FALSE)
```
:::


Plot the $K$ functions one by one:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(japanesepinesK)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Here we see the observed $\hat{K}(r)$ function within the envelope. This means the data follow CSR at every distance.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(redwoodK)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The redwoods show clumping at shorter distances and then randomness at longer distances.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(beiK)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


This shows intense clumping at all distances.

Thus, the `japanesepines` are randomly distributed while the other two are clumped but in different ways. The `redwood` data are clumped out to a radius of a fifth of the bounding box while the `bei` data are clustered out as far as we can measure. Oh, and how far can we measure before running into edge effects?

### Distances and edges
Edge effects are a perennial problem is spatial analysis. The question always nags at us: what's over the edge of the map? Edge effects arise because points outside the boundary can't be counted, right? And points near the edge have to be down weighted in some way because we have less information about them. When we do distance-based analysis we have to be conservative about edges and there are two conceptual ways of thinking about this. One, we can limit our analysis to short distances to reduce the number of edge cases. Two, we can try to correct for the edge effects. 

Look back the x-axis of the plots of $\hat K$ above. Note that the distances are much shorter than the span of the original data. Both the `japanesepines` and the `redwood` data are stored with the coordinate data on a scale from 0 to 1 (but the `japanesepines` data are on a 5.7 x 5.7 m square as discussed above) and the `bei` data are in a 1000 x 500 m rectangle. The plots above truncate at distances of 1/4 the shortest span of the data. This is a default in `spatstat` and fairly conservative. We can override that using the `rmax` argument. E.g., we could look at distances up to 50% of the study area.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(envelope(redwood, fun = Kest, nsim = 100,
              verbose=FALSE, rmax=0.5),main="")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Is this reasonable or responsible? Ripley himself suggested the rule of 0.25 the short span of the data. There are discussions about other approaches in the literature but going deep on edge corrections is a rabbit hole.

The other way of being careful about edges is to "correct" for the effect. These are similarly tough reading. I remember slogging through Goreaud's 1999 [paper](https://www.jstor.org/stable/3237072?seq=1#metadata_info_tab_contents)
which came out the year I took spatial analysis when I was a master's student. It's enough to be aware that there are several different edge corrections out there. The Further Reading section points to where you can go deeper.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
japanesepinesK2 <- Kest(japanesepines, verbose=FALSE, 
                       correction=c("none","isotropic","border"))
plot(japanesepinesK2)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


### L hat
I've dragged you through $\hat K$ and the plots above and now I'm going to tell you that I'm not a fan of the way the $\hat K(r)$ as a function of $r$ looks. I think those plots are hard to interpret because the random expectation is non-linear and that is hard to visualize. I prefer looking them transformed so that the y-axis and x-axis are on a 1:1 scale. This is referred to as $L$ and is a simple transformation of the $K$ function:

$$L(r) = \sqrt{(K(r)/\pi} $$

This has the effect that for a a completely random point pattern, the theoretical value of $L(r)=r$. We can calculate it using `Lest` rather than `Kest`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
redwoodL <- envelope(redwood, fun = Lest, nsim = n, verbose=FALSE)
plot(redwoodL)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Bivand goes the extra step (e.g., Fig 7.9) of plotting $\hat K(r) - \pi r^2$ on the y-axis to make the theoretical expectation be zero at every distance. I'll take a crack at it using `ggplot` because I'm that kind of geek. Since `redwoodK` is a `data.frame` this is pretty simple.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(redwoodK, mapping = aes(x=r, ymin = lo-pi*r^2, ymax=hi-pi*r^2)) +
  geom_ribbon(fill="grey",alpha=0.5) + 
  geom_line(mapping = aes(y=theo-pi*r^2),col="red", linetype="dashed") +
  geom_line(mapping = aes(y=obs-pi*r^2)) +
  labs(y=expression(K(r) - pi~r^2), x = "r") +
  ylim(-0.05,0.05) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


I find this plot to be the simplest to interpret (it's also the way I was taught which influences that probably), the $L$ function next, and the raw $K$ function the hardest. But I tend to use $L$ plots because they are bundled in `spatstat` and redoing all the plots takes more work.

### Marked point patterns
Let's look at something a little more subtle and interesting. The `longleaf` data are what is called a marked point pattern. This means that we know something else about a point besides its location. In this case we know the diameter of each stem. You can do some more interesting things with marked point patterns. 

The `longleaf` data are a marked point pattern meaning that we have some other information on the points besides their locations. In this case we have the diameters of the trees at breast height (DBH=1.4m above ground) which is standard measure in forestry. These are stored as `marks` in the `longleaf` object. Again, look at the help file: `?longleaf`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data(longleaf)
summary(longleaf)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Marked planar point pattern:  584 points
Average intensity 0.0146 points per square metre

Coordinates are given to 1 decimal place
i.e. rounded to the nearest multiple of 0.1 metres

marks are numeric, of type 'double'
Summary:
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   2.00    9.10   26.15   26.84   42.12   75.90 

Window: rectangle = [0, 200] x [0, 200] metres
Window area = 40000 square metres
Unit of length: 1 metre
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(longleaf)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Note in the plot that the points are sized according to their diameter. We can look at the diameters of all the trees. E.g.,


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hist(longleaf$marks)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Let's examine the spatial pattern of just the big trees (DBH > 50cm) which is moderately big for a Longleaf pine.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
bigTrees <- subset(longleaf, marks > 50)
plot(envelope(bigTrees, fun = Lest, nsim = n, verbose=FALSE),
     main = "Big Trees")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


So in contrast to the whole data set, we see that while the entire data set shows clustering, the big trees show CSR. That's kind of cool. We could start speculating about what ecological processes might drive that pattern.

Standard practice in forestry is to consider trees as adults when they have diameters of 30cm of greater.  Let's see if our understanding of the point patterns change by age class. 

First, we will make a new object that creates categories (`factor`) from the continuous (`numeric`) size class of DBH using the `cut` function:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
longleaf2 <- cut(longleaf, breaks=c(0,30,Inf), 
                 labels=c("Sapling","Adult"))
summary(longleaf2)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Marked planar point pattern:  584 points
Average intensity 0.0146 points per square metre

Coordinates are given to 1 decimal place
i.e. rounded to the nearest multiple of 0.1 metres

Multitype:
        frequency proportion intensity
Sapling       313  0.5359589  0.007825
Adult         271  0.4640411  0.006775

Window: rectangle = [0, 200] x [0, 200] metres
Window area = 40000 square metres
Unit of length: 1 metre
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(longleaf2)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


I think by looking at that plot that the adults are clustered and the saplings even more so. Let's investigate:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
adults <- subset(longleaf2, marks == "Adult",drop=TRUE)
plot(envelope(adults, fun = Lest, verbose=FALSE), main="Adult")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
saplings <- subset(longleaf2, marks == "Sapling",drop=TRUE)
plot(envelope(saplings, fun = Lest, verbose=FALSE), main="Sapling")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-2.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Quick aside, note that we were actually calling `subset.ppp` and `cut.ppp` above because the class `ppp` has methods for `subset` and `cut`.

In the plotted L functions above we definitely see that both age classes are clustered and the saplings much more so than the adults. But looking at the map I think we should see how those age classes interact. Because it looks to me like they are in different places. Let's plot the density map of the adults and put the points where the sapling occur on top.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
adults.den <- density(adults)
plot(adults.den)
points(saplings, pch=20)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


That is fascinating. Is it possible that the two age classes are repelled from each other?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
longleaf2L <- envelope(longleaf2, "Lcross", 
                       i ="Sapling",j = "Adult",
                       verbose=FALSE)
plot(longleaf2L)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-26-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


This shows the cross L-function between saplings and adults. The L-function is based on distances from points from one age class to points from the other class. Thus, the plot shows the L-function for saplings to adult pairs.This suggests that while there is within class attraction (the clustering seen previously), there is between class repulsion (inhibition). Can you put on your ecologist hat and figure out why this might be happening?  

## Roll Your Own
You can create your own point pattern by clicking on the screen and then plotting the kernel density and Ripley's K (or L). Try it several times, making different types of patterns: uniform, clumped, CSR. It's a useful calibration exercise -- CSR looks less random than most people expect.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
foo <- clickppp(n = 50) # 50 points
# KDE
plot(density(foo))
points(foo,pch=20)
# L
env <- envelope(foo, Lest, nsim = 100)
plot(env)
```
:::


Note that `clickppp` is interactive, so this won't run in a compiled document without some extra work (save with `saveRDS`, load with `readRDS`). It's worth doing at the console at least once.

## More Than Trees
The `spatstat` library has a ludicrous number of data sets to work with. If you are not interested in trees for some reason (weird, I know) you can look at point patterns for all kinds of things: anemones, ants, gorilla nests, mycorrhizal fungi, waterstriders. Type `demo(data)` at the prompt to see everything available.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
demo(data)
```
:::


Note the 3d data as well as the linear and dendritic point pattern data. Point pattern analysis goes places.

## Wrapping Up

KDE and Ripley's K are asking the same question from different angles: are these points distributed randomly, or is there structure? KDE shows you the density surface visually. Ripley's K gives you a statistical test against the null of complete spatial randomness. Used together, they're a pretty powerful first look at a point dataset.

What they can't tell you is why the pattern exists. Clustering could mean habitat preference, conspecific attraction, dispersal limitation, or just that the whole study area is better habitat than the surroundings. Figuring out which requires bringing in more data, more context, or a better question.

The next chapters move from point patterns to continuous variables: instead of asking where events cluster, we'll ask how a measured value like lead concentration or species richness varies across space. That's autocorrelation, and it's where most of the rest of the book lives.

## Exercises

### Simulated Data
Here is a point pattern object (class `ppp`) built from the "D" pattern in `simDat` above, with units of kilometers. Before plotting the L function, predict what you expect to see. Then run it. Is it what you anticipated? Try the other patterns too -- is "B" distinguishable from "C"?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
patternD <- simDat %>% filter(id=="D")
patternD <- ppp(x = patternD$x, y=patternD$y,
                xrange=c(0,1), yrange=c(0,1),
                unitname="km")
summary(patternD)
plot(patternD)
```
:::



### Mycorrhizal Fungi
Here is an example of three species of [ectomycorrhizae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ectomycorrhiza) distributed around a tree.

| ![Hebloma](assets/Hebloma.jpeg) | ![L_lac](assets/L_lac.jpeg) | ![L_pub](assets/L_pub.jpeg) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| *Hebloma crustuliniforme* | *Lactarius laccata* | *Lactarius pubescens* |



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data(sporophores) # run ?sporophores to see the help file. It's cool.
summary(sporophores)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Marked planar point pattern:  330 points
Average intensity 0.004998072 points per square cm

Coordinates are given to 15 decimal places

Multitype:
            frequency proportion    intensity
L laccata         190 0.57575760 0.0028776780
L pubescens        11 0.03333333 0.0001666024
Hebloma spp       129 0.39090910 0.0019537920

Window: polygonal boundary
single connected closed polygon with 128 vertices
enclosing rectangle: [-145, 145] x [-145, 145] cm
                     (290 x 290 cm)
Window area = 66025.5 square cm
Unit of length: 1 cm
Fraction of frame area: 0.785
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(sporophores, chars=c(16,1,2), cex=0.6, leg.args=list(cex=1.1))
points(0,0,pch=16, cex=2)
text(15,8,"Tree", cex=0.75)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03Point_Patterns_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-30-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


This is a marked point pattern with three species of fungi. The data come from @ford1980spatial. Different species have different functional roles and differing abilities to colonize. Sporophores are mushrooms, in case that wasn't obvious.

@ford1980spatial suggest two things we can investigate with PPA. First, that there "were distinct arcs around the tree which were not occupied by sporophores of any species." Second, that there is "no consistent evidence of spatial inhibition between species." We have one of the five years of data here. Is there any suggestion of inhibition between species?

To subset the data and run the cross L-function for two species, here are two equivalent approaches. The default plot for `alltypes` is essentially unreadable, so going pairwise is cleaner.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# using subset
heb_pub <- subset(sporophores,
                  marks %in% c("L pubescens","Hebloma spp"), drop=TRUE)
cross_heb_pub <- envelope(heb_pub, "Lcross", verbose=FALSE)
# or directly in envelope (note i and j arguments)
cross_heb_pub <- envelope(sporophores, "Lcross", i="L pubescens", j = "Hebloma spp", verbose=FALSE)
```
:::


Try changing the number of simulations. Try setting `global=TRUE` and see how that changes your understanding of what "significance" means here.

## Further Reading

@bivand2008pointpattern, chapter 7 through section 7.5, is the standard reference for point pattern analysis in R. It covers the theory behind the functions we used here and goes deeper on edge corrections and marked patterns. @fortin2016spatial is an accessible overview of spatial analysis broadly that pairs well with this chapter and the ones that follow.

If you want to see exactly what `Kest()` is computing under the hood, work through the [Ripley's K by Hand aside](03xAsideRipleyK.html). It derives K from scratch on a small dataset and checks the result against `spatstat`.
